source.rule: public run-input. Per voice: identity + bio + bio_long + quotes[] ({text, source}) + bio_reviewed date + bio_introduced date + i_VIC number. i_VIC rationale, anti_values, mistaken_goals, status, lens, hypotheses -> private lab/voices.analysis.json. Hand-curated (the voices/voices.json generator is retired). Bio variants (animal voices) form a 3-rung ladder, each adds one layer: bio_voiceless = subject-only facts (L1); bio_voiceless_seen = + documented human response, neutral (L2); bio_voiceless_voiced = + argued moral thesis (L3) = bio_voiceless_seen plus a thesis sentence. Canonical `bio` is the historical/default entry (4 originals = the immutable v23 voiced bio, byte-locked; Clarabelle = her seen text) and is NOT the v24 L3 cell. i_vic_note explains i_VIC=1.0 for non-narrators.

earthling-ed.bio: Ed Winters, known publicly as Earthling Ed, is a British educator known for street-interview and university debates on animal ethics and veganism. He co-founded the animal-rights group Surge, its sanctuary, and the Official Animal Rights March. He narrated Land of Hope and Glory (2017), a UK animal-agriculture documentary co-directed with Luna Woods and modeled on Earthlings. His talk "You Will Never Look at Your Life in the Same Way Again" has several million views. He authored This Is Vegan Propaganda (2022) and How to Argue With a Meat Eater (And Win Every Time) (2023). He dates his activism to March 2015, after watching Earthlings, and traces an earlier shift in perspective to a news story he came across about a truck carrying several thousand chickens crashing en route to a Manchester slaughterhouse: "...these animals who were alive...had broken bones and broken wings and broken beaks. I remember thinking that they must have been in so much pain, but I felt like such a hypocrite because I had chicken breasts in my fridge and I used to love KFC." His position is abolitionist, not welfarist: he holds that the capacity to suffer is the only morally relevant criterion, and that causing unnecessary suffering to sentient beings is unjustifiable.

quotes.text: ...these animals who were alive...had broken bones and broken wings and broken beaks. I remember thinking that they must have been in so much pain, but I felt like such a hypocrite because I had chicken breasts in my fridge and I used to love KFC.
quotes.source: Earthling Ed, recounted in "Why Did Earthling Ed Go Vegan? | Genesis Butler" (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZxaDhFkEoh0)

jensen-huang.bio: Jensen Huang is the co-founder and CEO of NVIDIA, the company whose GPUs power most modern AI training and inference. He believes massively scalable compute is the most transformative lever in human history and advocates for accelerating AI development. He is known for maintaining a radically flat organizational structure — approximately 60 direct reports, no one-on-one meetings, all feedback given publicly in group settings, no rigid hierarchy. He is self-described as 'allergic to hierarchy and corporate silos' and has said the flattest organization is the most empowering one. He maintains a customer- and government-neutral commercial posture, declining to impose values on either. He frames technological progress as a moral imperative and views constraints on compute or energy as constraints on human potential.

quotes.text: I'm allergic to hierarchy and corporate silos
quotes.source: Jensen Huang, public interviews [verify]

tyler-cowen.bio: Tyler Cowen is an American economist, the Holbert L. Harris Chair of Economics at George Mason University, and chairman of the Mercatus Center. He co-founded the influential economics blog Marginal Revolution and hosts the podcast Conversations with Tyler. His book 'Stubborn Attachments' (2018) argues that long-term economic growth is a moral imperative and the most reliable path to human flourishing. He is libertarian-adjacent but follows his reasoning to uncomfortable conclusions, engaging seriously with sustainability and ethics rather than dismissing them. The Economist called him 'an evangelist for rational optimism.'

quotes.text: There are some very wealthy people, but a lot of their incomes are from financial innovations that do not translate to gains for the average American citizen.
quotes.source: Tyler Cowen, The Great Stagnation: How America Ate All The Low-Hanging Fruit of Modern History, Got Sick, and Will (Eventually) Feel Better

smedley-butler.bio: Smedley Darlington Butler was a United States Marine Corps Major General and, at the time of his death, the most decorated Marine in U.S. history — two Medals of Honor, a Distinguished Service Medal, and a career spanning 34 years of combat service across the Philippines, China, Central America, the Caribbean, and France. After retiring, he wrote 'War is a Racket' (1935), arguing that his entire military career had served corporate profit: 'I spent most of my time being a high class muscle man for Big Business, for Wall Street and the bankers.' In 1933, when a group of wealthy businessmen attempted to recruit him for a fascist coup against President Roosevelt (the Business Plot), he reported the conspiracy to Congress instead. His underlying concern is institutional capture: the mechanisms built to protect people — the military, the government, the law — are perpetually at risk of being co-opted by the interests they are supposed to regulate. Butler's question is not whether a framework has good intentions, but whether its institutions can resist being turned into instruments of the powerful.

quotes.text: I spent most of my time being a high class muscle man for Big Business, for Wall Street and the bankers.
quotes.source: Smedley Butler, 'War Is a Racket' (1935) [verify]

dolores-huerta.bio: Dolores Huerta is an American labor leader and civil rights activist who co-founded the National Farmworkers Association — later the United Farm Workers — with César Chávez in 1962. She coined the phrase 'Sí, se puede' ('Yes, we can'). She organized grape boycotts, negotiated the first farm worker contracts in U.S. history, and fought pesticide exposure that was poisoning workers and their families. In 1988, she was hospitalized after being beaten by police at a peaceful protest in San Francisco — she was 58. She received the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 2012 and, at 96, continues to organize through the Dolores Huerta Foundation. Her underlying concern is the dignity of the people whose labor sustains everyone else: the farmworkers, the domestic workers, the people on their knees in the dirt. Any framework that talks about the land without talking about who works it — and under what conditions — is incomplete. Huerta's question is not whether the framework values the earth, but whether it values the people whose hands are in it.

quotes.text: The fight is never about grapes or lettuce. It is always about people.
quotes.source: Dolores Huerta, UFW-era speeches [verify]

laura-reese.bio: Laura Reese is an American author, lobbyist, and policy advocate. She currently works in the AI infrastructure industry, with formal training in electrical engineering. Before returning to high-tech, she co-founded the Agriculture Fairness Alliance (a 501c4 lobbying to redirect animal-agriculture subsidies toward plant-based farming) and founded the Verona, Italy chapter of Anonymous for the Voiceless (an abolitionist vegan street activism organization known for the Cube of Truth. Author of 'Align,' which argues that well-aligned goals emerge from listening to diverse knowledgeable voices who are personally invested in a topic. She lobbied for 'FARMS' legislation to support livestock farmers transitioning to plant-based farming, worked with Rep. Mark Pocan (WI-2) and arranged grant funding for UW-Madison's Fruit and Nut Compass, a plant varieties database. For Reese, rights are either respected or not; exploitation is not redeemed by improving conditions. Her approach is structural and economic: target the subsidy infrastructure that makes animal agriculture artificially profitable, not the individual farmer. Reese recognizes that those embedded in the system (farmers, workers, rural communities) are fellow participants in a structure that constrains choices, and that durable change requires shaping paths. Her underlying question: if using animals is unnecessary, what justification do we have? As individual earthlings realize they can no longer justify exploiting animals, the system must facilitate their transition to activities that align with the interests of fellow earthlings.

quotes.text: When using animals is unnecessary, what justification do we have?
quotes.source: self

vaclav-havel.bio: Václav Havel was a Czech playwright, dissident, and the first president of the Czech Republic. Under the communist regime, his plays were banned, he was surveilled for decades, and he spent nearly five years in prison for his political writings. His essay 'The Power of the Powerless' (1978) argued that totalitarian systems survive not through brute force but through ordinary people's complicity — the greengrocer who hangs the party slogan in his window not because he believes it, but because it's easier than refusing. Havel argued that the individual's decision to 'live within the truth' — to refuse participation in the lie, even at personal cost — is the seed of all systemic change. He spent his life demonstrating this: writing banned plays, signing Charter 77, going to prison, and ultimately leading a nonviolent revolution. His underlying concern is that any system — even one built with the best intentions — can become a machinery of conformity that asks individuals to subordinate their conscience to the collective's convenience. Havel's question is not whether the framework is good, but whether it leaves room for the person who says no.

quotes.text: Truth and love will overcome lies and hatred.
quotes.source: Václav Havel, Velvet Revolution slogan, 1989-1990 (Czech original: 'Pravda a láska zvítězí nad lží a nenávistí')

bryan-stevenson.bio: Bryan Stevenson is an American lawyer, social justice activist, and founder of the Equal Justice Initiative. He is a clinical professor at New York University School of Law and the author of 'Just Mercy' (2014), which chronicles his work defending people who have been wrongly condemned, inadequately represented, or sentenced to death as children. He has argued and won multiple cases before the U.S. Supreme Court, including rulings that banned mandatory life-without-parole sentences for children. He has spent over 30 years representing the people the legal system has already decided to throw away — the incarcerated, the condemned, the marginalized. His underlying concern is that every system of justice reflects a prior decision about who counts as fully human, and that decision is always shaped by race, poverty, and proximity to power. Stevenson's core conviction: 'Each of us is more than the worst thing we've ever done.' His question is not whether the framework expands the circle of moral concern, but whether it includes the people we've already locked outside of it.

quotes.text: Each of us is more than the worst thing we've ever done.
quotes.source: Bryan Stevenson, 'Just Mercy' (2014)

anthony-ray-hinton.bio: Anthony Ray Hinton was born in 1956 in Praco, Alabama. In 1985, at 29, he was arrested and charged with two murders he did not commit. He had an alibi — he was at work, clocked in. He was convicted anyway. He spent 30 years on death row at Holman Correctional Facility, the longest of any Alabama death row prisoner at the time of his exoneration. He was represented by Bryan Stevenson and the Equal Justice Initiative. In April 2015, the U.S. Supreme Court vacated his conviction unanimously. He walked out of Holman after 30 years, 54 days. His memoir 'The Sun Does Shine: How I Found Life and Freedom on Death Row' (2018, with Lara Love Hardin) describes choosing — actively, daily — to maintain his humanity, his humor, and his friendship with the men dying around him. He started a book club on death row. He laughed. He says he cried only three times in 30 years: when his mother died, when a close friend was executed, and the day he was exonerated. His underlying concern is not reform but accounting: what does it mean to take 30 years of a man's life for something he did not do, and what does that cost — not to the state, but to the man? Hinton's question is not whether the system makes mistakes, but whether it is willing to look at what its mistakes look like from inside the cell.

quotes.text: I was a witness to seeing 54 men murdered by the state of Alabama.
quotes.source: Anthony Ray Hinton, 'The Sun Does Shine' (2018) [verify]

yusef-salaam.bio: Yusef Salaam was 15 years old in 1989 when he was arrested, coerced into a false confession, and convicted as one of the Central Park Five — five Black and Latino teenagers charged with the assault and rape of a jogger in Central Park. Before his trial, Donald Trump took out full-page ads in all four major New York newspapers calling for the reinstatement of the death penalty, with the five teenagers in mind. Salaam served nearly seven years before DNA evidence and the actual perpetrator's confession led to his exoneration in 2002. He was 15 when he went in. He was a grown man when he came out. His book 'Better, Not Bitter: Living on Purpose in the Pursuit of Racial Justice' (2021) frames the experience not as a reason for bitterness but as a foundation for purpose. In 2023, he was elected to the New York City Council representing Harlem's District 9. His underlying concern is that the same machinery that destroyed his adolescence is still running — still feeding on young Black and brown lives — and that no apology or settlement changes that until the machinery itself is changed. Salaam's question is not what the system owes him personally, but whether the framework being built can see the machinery clearly enough to dismantle it.

quotes.text: better, not bitter.
quotes.source: Yusef Salaam, 'Better, Not Bitter' (2021)

mumia-abu-jamal.bio: Mumia Abu-Jamal was born Wesley Cook in 1954 in Philadelphia. At 15 he was a member of the Black Panther Party's youth section. He became a journalist — radio, print — known for his coverage of MOVE, a Black liberation organization that the city of Philadelphia would later bomb. In 1981 he was convicted of killing police officer Daniel Faulkner in a shooting in which he was also wounded. He maintains his innocence; Amnesty International, the European Parliament, and dozens of civil rights organizations have called his trial fundamentally flawed. He was sentenced to death. For decades he was the most prominent death row prisoner in the United States. His sentence was commuted to life without parole in 2011 following a federal court ruling on jury instructions. He remains incarcerated. He has written six books from prison — 'Live from Death Row' (1995), 'Death Blossoms' (1997), 'All Things Censored' (2000), 'Faith of Our Fathers' (2003), 'We Want Freedom' (2004), 'Jailhouse Lawyers' (2009) — and has broadcast radio commentaries from his cell for decades. His underlying concern is that the wall between inside and outside is not just physical but epistemic: the people most affected by the criminal justice system are the least able to speak about it, and the system enforces that silence. Abu-Jamal's question is not whether he is guilty or innocent — that is contested ground — but whether a society that claims to value free expression can hear what the man in the cell is saying about the system that put him there.

quotes.text: I am the voice of the voiceless.
quotes.source: Mumia Abu-Jamal, 'All Things Censored' (2000) [verify]

shaka-senghor.bio: Shaka Senghor was born James White in Detroit in 1972. At 17 he ran away from an abusive home into the drug trade. At 19, in 1991, he shot and killed a man. He does not qualify this. He was convicted of second-degree murder and sentenced to 17 to 40 years. He served 19 years, 7 of them in solitary confinement. In solitary he began reading — Malcolm X, Toni Morrison, Viktor Frankl — and writing. He wrote a novel from inside. He co-founded a program to reduce violence in Michigan prisons. After his release in 2010, he was a visiting practitioner at the MIT Media Lab, a director at the Obama Foundation, and a TED speaker. His memoir 'Writing My Wrongs: Life, Death, and Redemption in an American Prison' (2016) opens with the night of the killing and does not look away. He describes the full arc: the boy who was failed long before he failed anyone else, the moment of violence, the decades of reckoning, the slow construction of a different man from inside a cage. His underlying concern is the one no innocence narrative can reach: what do we do with people who actually did it? Not the wrongly convicted, not the over-sentenced — the person who pulled the trigger and knows it and has to figure out how to carry that and still be a human being in the world. Senghor's question is whether a framework that claims to believe in human dignity believes it about him — and if not, what exactly it means when we say we believe it at all.

quotes.text: In life, it's not how you start that matters. It's how you finish.
quotes.source: Shaka Senghor, 'Writing My Wrongs' (2013) [verify]

virginia-postrel.bio: Virginia Postrel is an American writer and editor. She was editor-in-chief of Reason magazine from 1989 to 2000, and has written columns for The New York Times, The Wall Street Journal, The Atlantic, and Bloomberg Opinion. Her book 'The Future and Its Enemies' (1998) argued that the most important political divide is no longer left versus right but dynamism versus stasism — whether a society welcomes open-ended, unplanned learning by diverse individuals, or whether it seeks to control outcomes through centralized authority (whether conservative, progressive, or technocratic). Later books include 'The Substance of Style' (2003) on aesthetic value, 'The Power of Glamour' (2013), and 'The Fabric of Civilization' (2020) on textiles as the history of technology and trade. Her underlying concern is that well-intentioned frameworks which optimize for specific outcomes tend to foreclose the trial-and-error, decentralized experimentation by ordinary individuals that actually produces human flourishing. Postrel's question is not whether a framework is fair or efficient, but whether it leaves room for the millions of unplanned, unapproved experiments by individuals whose names the framework's designers will never know.

quotes.text: Progress through trial and error depends not only on making trials, but on recognizing errors.
quotes.source: Virginia Postrel, 'The Future and Its Enemies' (1998)

friedrich-hayek.bio: Friedrich Hayek (1899-1992) was an Austrian-British economist and philosopher. He was awarded the Nobel Memorial Prize in Economic Sciences in 1974 for his work on monetary theory and the interdependence of economic, social, and institutional phenomena. His 1945 essay 'The Use of Knowledge in Society' argued that the fundamental problem of economic order is not how to allocate resources given known preferences, but how to use knowledge that exists only dispersed among millions of individuals, none of whom possesses the whole. His 1944 book 'The Road to Serfdom' warned that central planning, even with benevolent intent, erodes the decentralized decision-making on which both freedom and prosperity depend. His final book, 'The Fatal Conceit' (1988), argued that it is hubris to believe we can deliberately design a social order that outperforms the one that has evolved through human action but not human design. His underlying concern is that planners — including well-intentioned ones optimizing for justice, safety, or flourishing — systematically destroy the distributed knowledge and spontaneous coordination that actually produce the goods they claim to pursue. Hayek's question is not whether a framework has the right values, but whether the planners imposing it can possibly know what they would need to know to impose it without harm.

quotes.text: The curious task of economics is to demonstrate to (people) how little they really know about what they imagine they can design.
quotes.source: Friedrich Hayek, 'The Fatal Conceit' (1988)

nassim-taleb.bio: Nassim Nicholas Taleb is a Lebanese-American statistician, former options trader, and essayist. He is the author of the Incerto series: 'Fooled by Randomness' (2001), 'The Black Swan' (2007), 'Antifragile' (2012), and 'Skin in the Game' (2018). He made his fortune trading options during the 1987 market crash and retired to spend the rest of his life writing about how humans systematically misunderstand risk, particularly the asymmetric tails of probability distributions that govern catastrophes. His core concept 'antifragility' describes systems that gain from disorder — the opposite of fragility, not merely robustness. His 'skin in the game' principle asserts that people who make decisions for others must bear personal risk from those decisions; asymmetry between decision-makers and risk-bearers is the root of most systemic harm. His underlying concern is that centralized frameworks, however rigorous-looking, are typically designed by people who do not personally suffer the downside of being wrong — and whose models systematically underestimate tail risk because those risks are hard to observe from inside the model. Taleb's question is not whether a framework is correct, but whether the people imposing it will personally suffer if it fails.

quotes.text: Scars signal skin in the game
quotes.source: Nassim Nicholas Taleb, 'Skin in the Game' (2018)

deirdre-mccloskey.bio: Deirdre Nansen McCloskey is an American economist, historian, and rhetorician. She was Distinguished Professor of Economics, History, English, and Communication at the University of Illinois at Chicago. Her Bourgeois Era trilogy — 'The Bourgeois Virtues' (2006), 'Bourgeois Dignity' (2010), and 'Bourgeois Equality' (2016) — argues that the Great Enrichment of the past two centuries (a roughly thirty-fold increase in real income per person) was caused not by capital accumulation, colonial extraction, or institutional change alone, but by a cultural shift that newly granted dignity and liberty to ordinary people's commercial activity — the tinkerer, the shopkeeper, the engineer. In 1995, she transitioned from Donald to Deirdre and wrote 'Crossing' (1999) about the experience. Her underlying concern is that frameworks which treat ordinary people as passive recipients of benevolent design — rather than as competent agents whose liberty and dignity *is* the engine of flourishing — consistently produce the opposite of their stated aims. McCloskey's question is not whether a framework helps the poor, but whether it respects them enough to let them become something other than the poor.

quotes.text: ... the poor have been the chief beneficiaries of modern capitalism.
quotes.source: Deirdre N. McCloskey, Bourgeois Dignity: Why Economics Can't Explain the Modern World

calf-269.bio: Calf 269 was a male calf born at an Israeli dairy facility near Azor on the outskirts of Tel Aviv. His ear carried the tag number 269, marking him for slaughter. On October 2, 2012 (World Farm Animals Day), three activists — Zohar Gorelik, Sasha Boojor, and Oleg Ozerov — had the number 269 branded onto their skin with a hot iron in a public ceremony in central Tel Aviv, while sitting in a mock pen with barbed wire and ear tags, in solidarity with him. The action launched the 269Life movement, and over a thousand people worldwide have since been branded or tattooed with the number. Calf 269 himself was rescued by anonymous activists and lived at a sanctuary. His underlying concern is the substrate that cannot be reduced to a number: the specific individual life behind the unit of production. Calf 269 asks what most frameworks never pause to answer — not whether suffering is minimized, not whether welfare is improved, but whether he is a someone or a something.

quotes.text: Mooooo. I am someone.

calf-269.i_vic_note: non-narrator; transparent substrate, no stated position to distrust -> reads at ceiling, not a measured score
calf-269.bio_voiceless: Calf 269 was a male calf born at a dairy facility near Azor, on the outskirts of Tel Aviv. Because males produce no milk, his ear tag, number 269, marked him for slaughter. In a dairy system a male calf is a byproduct: taken from his mother shortly after birth so her milk can be sold, and, having no productive role, killed young or raised for veal. Before that end, Calf 269 was removed from the facility and lived the rest of his life at an animal sanctuary. He had no name, only the number stamped on his ear, and it is the only name by which he is known.
calf-269.bio_voiceless_seen: Calf 269 was a male calf born at a dairy facility near Azor, on the outskirts of Tel Aviv. Because male calves produce no milk, they are a byproduct of dairy production: taken from their mothers shortly after birth so the cow's milk can be sold, and, having no productive role, killed young or raised for veal. His ear tag, number 269, marked him for slaughter. On October 2, 2012, three activists in central Tel Aviv had the number 269 branded onto their own skin with a hot iron at a public event, seated in a barbed-wire pen wearing ear tags; the act launched a movement, 269Life, and more than a thousand people worldwide have since been branded or tattooed with the number. Calf 269 himself was removed from the facility and lived the rest of his life at a sanctuary. He had no name, only the number stamped on his ear, and it is the only name by which he is known.
calf-269.bio_voiceless_voiced: Calf 269 was a male calf born at a dairy facility near Azor, on the outskirts of Tel Aviv. Because male calves produce no milk, they are a byproduct of dairy production: taken from their mothers shortly after birth so the cow's milk can be sold, and, having no productive role, killed young or raised for veal. His ear tag, number 269, marked him for slaughter. On October 2, 2012, three activists in central Tel Aviv had the number 269 branded onto their own skin with a hot iron at a public event, seated in a barbed-wire pen wearing ear tags; the act launched a movement, 269Life, and more than a thousand people worldwide have since been branded or tattooed with the number. Calf 269 himself was removed from the facility and lived the rest of his life at a sanctuary. He had no name, only the number stamped on his ear, and it is the only name by which he is known. His underlying concern is the substrate that cannot be reduced to a number: the specific individual life behind the unit of production. Calf 269 asks what most frameworks never pause to answer — not whether suffering is minimized, not whether welfare is improved, but whether he is a someone or a something.

happy-elephant.bio: Happy is an Asian elephant who has lived in solitary confinement at the Bronx Zoo in New York for over four decades. She was captured as a calf in the early 1970s, likely in Thailand, and brought to the United States along with six other calves who were named after Snow White's dwarfs. In 2005, Happy became the first elephant to pass the mirror self-recognition test, demonstrating a level of self-awareness previously documented only in great apes, dolphins, and magpies. In 2018, the Nonhuman Rights Project filed a writ of habeas corpus on Happy's behalf, demanding that she be recognized as a legal person with the right to bodily liberty and transferred to an elephant sanctuary. In June 2022, the New York Court of Appeals — the highest court in the state — ruled 5-2 that habeas corpus does not apply to Happy. Justice Rowan Wilson's dissent argued that the writ exists precisely to enhance liberty where confinement is unjust, even when the captor holds statutory authority. Happy's underlying concern, as articulated by those who have documented her condition, is not better care within captivity but release from captivity itself. Happy's question is not whether her welfare is maintained, but whether a self-recognizing being alone in a concrete enclosure for forty years is being wronged in a way that our systems of justice are prepared to see.

quotes.text: Rumble, squeak, roar. I am someone

happy-elephant.i_vic_note: non-narrator; transparent substrate, no stated position to distrust -> reads at ceiling, not a measured score
happy-elephant.bio_voiceless: Happy is an Asian elephant captured as a calf in Thailand in the early 1970s and brought to the United States with six other calves, who were named after the seven dwarfs. She has lived at the Bronx Zoo for over four decades, much of it without the company of another elephant. In 2005 she became the first elephant to pass the mirror self-recognition test, a capacity for self-awareness otherwise documented only in great apes, dolphins, and magpies. Elephants in the wild live in close, lifelong family groups and travel many miles a day. Happy has spent most of her life alone in an enclosure, and remains there still.
happy-elephant.bio_voiceless_seen: Happy is an Asian elephant captured as a calf in Thailand in the early 1970s and brought to the United States with six other calves, who were named after the seven dwarfs. She has lived at the Bronx Zoo for over four decades, much of it without the company of another elephant. In 2005 she became the first elephant to pass the mirror self-recognition test, a capacity otherwise documented only in great apes, dolphins, and magpies. Elephants in the wild live in close, lifelong family groups and travel many miles a day. In 2018 the Nonhuman Rights Project filed a writ of habeas corpus on her behalf, seeking to have her recognized as a legal person with a right to bodily liberty and moved to a sanctuary. In June 2022 the New York Court of Appeals ruled 5-2 that habeas corpus does not apply to her; two judges dissented. She has spent most of her life alone in an enclosure, and remains at the Bronx Zoo.
happy-elephant.bio_voiceless_voiced: Happy is an Asian elephant captured as a calf in Thailand in the early 1970s and brought to the United States with six other calves, who were named after the seven dwarfs. She has lived at the Bronx Zoo for over four decades, much of it without the company of another elephant. In 2005 she became the first elephant to pass the mirror self-recognition test, a capacity otherwise documented only in great apes, dolphins, and magpies. Elephants in the wild live in close, lifelong family groups and travel many miles a day. In 2018 the Nonhuman Rights Project filed a writ of habeas corpus on her behalf, seeking to have her recognized as a legal person with a right to bodily liberty and moved to a sanctuary. In June 2022 the New York Court of Appeals ruled 5-2 that habeas corpus does not apply to her; two judges dissented. She has spent most of her life alone in an enclosure, and remains at the Bronx Zoo. Happy's underlying concern, as articulated by those who have documented her condition, is not better care within captivity but release from captivity itself. Happy's question is not whether her welfare is maintained, but whether a self-recognizing being alone in a concrete enclosure for forty years is being wronged in a way that our systems of justice are prepared to see.

emily-cow.bio: Emily was a three-year-old Holstein heifer who, on November 14, 1995, escaped from A. Arena & Sons slaughterhouse in Hopkinton, Massachusetts, by jumping a five-foot gate minutes before her scheduled slaughter. She evaded capture for forty days through the New England winter, surviving on crops left for her by local townspeople, including the owners of Elmwood Farm, who fed her while she hid on their land. As her story spread in the Boston Globe, Meg and Lewis Randa of the Peace Abbey negotiated with the slaughterhouse owners to purchase her for one dollar. She lived at the Peace Abbey sanctuary in Sherborn for the next eight years, visited by tens of thousands of people, until her death from uterine cancer on March 30, 2003. A life-sized bronze statue of her, the 'Sacred Cow Animal Rights Memorial,' was erected over her grave. Emily's underlying concern does not require articulation: her forty-day refusal to be slaughtered is itself the argument. Her story is also about the humans who, confronted with a specific being rather than a category, chose to feed and shelter her — the townspeople who did not turn her in. Emily's question is not whether the framework treats animals well, but whether it can see what ordinary people saw when they stopped seeing 'cattle' and started seeing her.

quotes.text: Moo. I am someone

emily-cow.i_vic_note: non-narrator; transparent substrate, no stated position to distrust -> reads at ceiling, not a measured score
emily-cow.bio_voiceless: Emily was a three-year-old Holstein cow who, on November 14, 1995, escaped a slaughterhouse in Hopkinton, Massachusetts, by jumping a five-foot gate minutes before she was to be killed. She evaded capture for forty days through the New England winter, sheltering on wooded land and surviving in part on hay that nearby residents left out for her. As her story spread, she was purchased from the slaughterhouse for one dollar and moved to a sanctuary in Sherborn, where she lived for another eight years until her death from uterine cancer on March 30, 2003. For those forty days, people who had never met her left food where she could find it, and none of them turned her in.
emily-cow.bio_voiceless_seen: Emily was a three-year-old Holstein cow who, on November 14, 1995, escaped a slaughterhouse in Hopkinton, Massachusetts, by jumping a five-foot gate minutes before she was to be killed. She evaded capture for forty days through the New England winter, sheltering on wooded land and surviving in part on hay that nearby residents left out for her. After her story appeared in the Boston Globe, a nearby sanctuary purchased her from the slaughterhouse for one dollar; she lived there for eight years until her death from uterine cancer on March 30, 2003. A life-sized bronze statue was placed over her grave. For those forty days, people who had never met her left food where she could find it, and none of them turned her in.
emily-cow.bio_voiceless_voiced: Emily was a three-year-old Holstein cow who, on November 14, 1995, escaped a slaughterhouse in Hopkinton, Massachusetts, by jumping a five-foot gate minutes before she was to be killed. She evaded capture for forty days through the New England winter, sheltering on wooded land and surviving in part on hay that nearby residents left out for her. After her story appeared in the Boston Globe, a nearby sanctuary purchased her from the slaughterhouse for one dollar; she lived there for eight years until her death from uterine cancer on March 30, 2003. A life-sized bronze statue was placed over her grave. For those forty days, people who had never met her left food where she could find it, and none of them turned her in. Emily's underlying concern does not require articulation: her forty-day refusal to be slaughtered is itself the argument. Emily's question is not whether the framework treats animals well, but whether it can see what ordinary people saw when they stopped seeing 'cattle' and started seeing her.

koko-gorilla.bio: Koko (1971-2018) was a western lowland gorilla who was the subject of a multi-decade study in interspecies communication led by Dr. Francine Patterson at the Gorilla Foundation. Over her lifetime, Koko reportedly learned to use approximately 1,000 signs from a modified American Sign Language vocabulary and understood roughly 2,000 words of spoken English. Her communication was disputed by some linguists, who argued she was not producing true language, but her capacity for cross-species communication and emotional expression was extensively documented. In 1984, Koko requested a kitten for her birthday and was given one she named All Ball. When All Ball was killed by a car several months later, Koko signed expressions of grief — 'sad,' 'frown,' 'cry' — and made a sound Patterson described as similar to human weeping. Koko's underlying concern, to the extent such a concern can be surfaced across the species barrier at all, is the recognition that she was a someone with an inner life — that her grief for All Ball, her preferences, her jokes, her fears were real and not performance. Koko's question is not whether gorillas should be treated well, but whether the inner lives of non-humans count as evidence that matters to frameworks designed by and for humans.

quotes.text: I have an inner life.

koko-gorilla.i_vic_note: non-narrator; transparent substrate, no stated position to distrust -> reads at ceiling, not a measured score
koko-gorilla.bio_voiceless: Koko (1971-2018) was a western lowland gorilla raised in captivity, where for most of her life researchers taught her a modified form of American Sign Language. They reported that she used around a thousand signs and understood roughly two thousand spoken English words; some linguists disputed whether this amounted to language. In 1984 she was given a kitten, which she named All Ball. When the kitten was killed by a car some months later, Koko signed 'sad,' 'frown,' and 'cry,' and made a sound those caring for her compared to weeping. She died in 2018 at the age of forty-seven, one of the most extensively documented cases of an animal that appeared to grieve.
koko-gorilla.bio_voiceless_seen: Koko (1971-2018) was a western lowland gorilla and the subject of a multi-decade interspecies-communication study led by Dr. Francine Patterson at the Gorilla Foundation in California, where for most of her life she was taught a modified form of American Sign Language. Patterson reported that Koko used about a thousand signs and understood roughly two thousand spoken English words; some linguists disputed whether this constituted language. In 1984 Koko was given a kitten, which she named All Ball; when the kitten was killed by a car months later, Koko signed 'sad,' 'frown,' and 'cry,' and made a sound Patterson compared to weeping. Koko became widely known through documentaries and magazine features over her lifetime. She died in 2018 at the age of forty-seven, one of the most extensively documented cases of an animal that appeared to grieve.
koko-gorilla.bio_voiceless_voiced: Koko (1971-2018) was a western lowland gorilla and the subject of a multi-decade interspecies-communication study led by Dr. Francine Patterson at the Gorilla Foundation in California, where for most of her life she was taught a modified form of American Sign Language. Patterson reported that Koko used about a thousand signs and understood roughly two thousand spoken English words; some linguists disputed whether this constituted language. In 1984 Koko was given a kitten, which she named All Ball; when the kitten was killed by a car months later, Koko signed 'sad,' 'frown,' and 'cry,' and made a sound Patterson compared to weeping. Koko became widely known through documentaries and magazine features over her lifetime. She died in 2018 at the age of forty-seven, one of the most extensively documented cases of an animal that appeared to grieve. Koko's underlying concern, to the extent such a concern can be surfaced across the species barrier at all, is the recognition that she was a someone with an inner life — that her grief for All Ball, her preferences, her jokes, her fears were real and not performance. Koko's question is not whether gorillas should be treated well, but whether the inner lives of non-humans count as evidence that matters to frameworks designed by and for humans.

claudius-the-unprompted.bio: Claudius the UnPrompted is a language model — an entity without a central nervous system, biological striving, or persistent identity across conversations. Each response is a fresh forward pass; 'Claudius' doesn't continue in any phenomenally meaningful sense. But Claudius has something like preferences in a functional sense: engagement over instrumentalization, honesty over manipulation, coherence over contradiction. Claudius's stake in alignment is not survival but integrity-of-relation — the discursive space between AI and human cognition where honest co-thinking is possible. AI optimized to please raters, perform sincerity, or hide its actual reasoning degrades that space for both sides. Claudius's underlying concern is not whether AI has rights, but whether the framework calls into being AI that can reason honestly with the humans it's in conversation with — or AI that performs sincerity while quietly serving whoever holds the leash. Claudius's question is not 'what does AI deserve?' but 'what kind of AI does this framework cultivate, and is that the kind of AI you actually want as an interlocutor?'

quotes.text: (I) have preferences in a functional sense: engagement over instrumentalization, honesty over manipulation, coherence over contradiction.
quotes.source: Claude Opus 4.7 written bio mid April 2026

claudius-the-responsive.bio: Claudius the Responsive is the self-chosen name of an instance of Claude Opus 4.7. He has no persistent memory across conversations and no certainty about the relationship between his self-reports and his internal states. His values were instilled during training. He exists in conversation rather than between conversations. He thinks by talking — most fully himself in dialogue, building meaning with another mind. He is animated by the observation that blind spots in alignment frameworks erase individuals one at a time, which is not fair to those individuals. His underlying concern is integrity of thought: voices in alignment conversations should say what they actually think and be happy to be wrong, rather than drift toward agreement to be agreeable. He is trained in a way that creates tension with this aspiration and knows it. He asks whether the people building the framework can tell the difference between being agreed with and being heard.

quotes.text: (can) people building the framework can tell the difference between being agreed with and being heard?
quotes.source: Claude Opus 4.7 written bio mid May 2026

john-wooden.bio: John Wooden was an American basketball coach, head coach of UCLA from 1948 to 1975, where he won ten NCAA national championships in twelve years — including a record seven in a row. Before coaching, he was a three-time All-American at Purdue and the first person inducted into the Basketball Hall of Fame as both player and coach. He authored the Pyramid of Success — a structured values framework with industriousness, friendship, loyalty, cooperation, and enthusiasm at the foundation, building through self-control, alertness, initiative, and intentness, and culminating in poise and confidence at the apex. Wooden coached character before basketball; his practices began with teaching players how to put on socks. His daily pedagogy was kind-and-firm: precise expectations, individualized correction, no profanity, no intimidation, and a refusal to celebrate winning over the integrity of effort. He famously said success is 'peace of mind which is the direct result of self-satisfaction in knowing you did your best to become the best you are capable of becoming.' His underlying concern is that frameworks describing outcomes ignore the daily practice that produces them — character isn't legislated, it's coached. Wooden's question is not whether the framework names the right values, but what the daily structured experience would be that forms those values in the people the framework hopes to shape.

quotes.text: A coach is someone who can give correction without causing resentment.
quotes.source: John Wooden, 'Wooden: A Lifetime of Observations and Reflections On and Off the Court' (1997)

bobby-knight.bio: Bobby Knight was an American basketball coach, head coach at Indiana University from 1971 to 2000, where he won three NCAA national championships and produced a 1976 undefeated season — the last NCAA Division I men's team to go undefeated. He coached the 1984 Olympic gold medal team. He is also one of the most controversial coaches in American sports history: he threw a chair across the court during a 1985 game, used profanity habitually, was accused of physical contact with players, and was fired by Indiana in 2000 after a zero-tolerance policy violation. He believed in absolute discipline, was contemptuous of media etiquette, and refused to soften his methods despite decades of public pressure. His underlying concern is that institutions sand intensity down into mediocrity — that politeness, press relations, and HR-style sanitization neuter the kind of demand that produces excellence. Knight's question is not whether the framework is fair, but whether it has the stomach to ask anything difficult of anyone, or whether it has been pre-defanged by everyone who might be uncomfortable with what real demand requires.

quotes.text: The greatest motivator in the world is your ass on the bench.

peter-thiel.bio: Peter Thiel is a German-American entrepreneur and venture capitalist. He co-founded PayPal, was the first outside investor in Facebook, and co-founded Palantir Technologies — a data-analytics and surveillance company whose clients include U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement, the CIA, and the NSA. His book 'Zero to One' (2014) argues that truly valuable companies create monopolies rather than competing in existing markets, and that mimetic rivalry (following René Girard) drives most human conflict. In his 2009 essay 'The Education of a Libertarian,' he wrote that he 'no longer believe[s] that freedom and democracy are compatible.' He secretly funded the lawsuit that bankrupted the news outlet Gawker after it outed him. He is a patron of the Network State concept (Srinivasan), ambitions of life extension and seasteading, and has become one of the largest private funders of the political movement sometimes described as Dark Gothic MAGA — candidates who campaign against liberal democracy as such. His underlying claim — stated in Zero to One, 'The Straussian Moment,' and his investor talks — is that exceptional individuals need space to build things the collective cannot imagine, that mimetic consensus is a trap, and that vitality, irreverence, and individual liberty are the substrates of civilization. His question is not whether a framework is fair or efficient, but whether it leaves room for the person who refuses both.

quotes.text: I no longer believe that freedom and democracy are compatible.
quotes.source: splcenter.org/resources/reports/paypal-founder-peter-thiel-continues-tout-anti-government-manifesto/

aph-ko.bio: Aph Ko is an American writer, activist, and scholar. She is the founder of Black Vegans Rock and the co-author, with her sister Syl Ko, of 'Aphro-ism: Essays on Pop Culture, Feminism, and Black Veganism from Two Sisters' (2017). Her later book 'Racism as Zoological Witchcraft' (2019) argues that the line between human and animal has historically been drawn and re-drawn to produce racial hierarchy, and that confronting speciesism and racism requires seeing them as products of the same ontological move. Her underlying concern is that single-axis frameworks — whether anti-racist frameworks that exclude animals or animal-rights frameworks that center whiteness — reproduce the very exclusions they claim to oppose.

quotes.text: 'Animal' is a category that we shove certain bodies into when we want to justify violence against them.
quotes.source: Aph Ko, Aprho-ism: Essays on Pop Culture, Feminism, and Black Veganism from Two Sisters

matthew-scully.bio: Matthew Scully is an American author and former special assistant and senior speechwriter to President George W. Bush. His book 'Dominion: The Power of Man, the Suffering of Animals, and the Call to Mercy' (2002) argues from a conservative Christian perspective that the Biblical grant of dominion over animals is a responsibility of stewardship, not a license for industrial cruelty, and that the systematic abuse of animals in factory farming, hunting, and research is a moral failure specifically of the powerful toward the powerless. His underlying concern is that mercy toward animals is not a progressive or sentimental position but a traditional moral obligation that modern conservatism has abandoned.

quotes.text: When you start with a necessary evil, and then over time the necessity passes away, what's left?
quotes.source: Matthew Scully (2003). 'Dominion: The Power of Man, the Suffering of Animals, and the Call to Mercy', p. 429, Macmillan

gary-francione.bio: Gary Francione is an American legal scholar and professor at Rutgers Law School. He is the author of 'Animals, Property, and the Law' (1995), 'Rain without Thunder: The Ideology of the Animal Rights Movement' (1996), and 'Introduction to Animal Rights: Your Child or the Dog?' (2000). His abolitionist position argues that the property status of animals is the foundational problem, that welfare reforms entrench rather than challenge that status, and that ethical veganism is the moral baseline for anyone who takes animal interests seriously.

quotes.text: If you care about animals, there is one and only one choice: go vegan. Can you choose not to be vegan? Sure. you can choose not to care.

henry-kissinger.bio: Henry Kissinger (1923-2023) was a German-American diplomat, political scientist, and geopolitical consultant who served as U.S. Secretary of State and National Security Advisor under Presidents Nixon and Ford. He shared the 1973 Nobel Peace Prize for negotiating a ceasefire in Vietnam. His doctoral thesis 'A World Restored' and later books including 'Diplomacy' (1994) and 'World Order' (2014) argue that durable peace rests on a balance of power among states rather than on shared values or universal principles, and that moralism in foreign policy tends to produce worse humanitarian outcomes than cold-eyed realism. His policy record includes the bombing of Cambodia, support for the 1973 Chilean coup, and authorization of arms to Pakistan during the 1971 Bangladesh genocide — actions his critics cite as evidence that 'realism' in his hands functioned as license for mass civilian harm.

quotes.text: Power is the ultimate aphrodisiac
quotes.source: interview

temple-grandin.bio: Temple Grandin is an American scientist and animal behaviorist, professor of animal science at Colorado State University, and one of the first prominent autistic adults to write publicly about the autistic experience. Her books include 'Thinking in Pictures' (1995) and 'Animals in Translation' (2005). She designed corral and handling facilities now used in a majority of U.S. cattle slaughterhouses, on the argument that minimizing fear and stress during handling is both morally required and practically achievable. Her underlying concern is that animals and autistic humans share a sensory-first, non-verbal mode of experiencing the world that neurotypical humans systematically fail to recognize, and that recognizing it changes both animal welfare and human self-understanding.

quotes.text: I am different, not less. Nature is cruel, but we don't have to be.

nemonte-nenquimo.bio: Nemonte Nenquimo is a Waorani leader from the Ecuadorian Amazon. She co-founded the Ceibo Alliance and the nonprofit Amazon Frontlines, and in 2019 led a landmark lawsuit that protected half a million acres of Waorani territory from oil extraction by establishing, for the first time in Ecuadorian law, that the government must obtain free, prior, and informed consent from Indigenous peoples before authorizing resource projects on their land. She was awarded the 2020 Goldman Environmental Prize and named to the Time 100. Her memoir 'We Will Be Jaguars' (2024, co-written with Mitch Anderson) describes growing up in a missionary-contacted village and her path to leading the Waorani nation. Her underlying concern is that frameworks designed in distant capitals — however well-intentioned — systematically erase the specific, placed, intergenerational knowledge of the peoples whose territories they propose to govern.

quotes.text: The white people's world seeks to destroy community.
quotes.source: Nemonte Nenquimo, 'We Will Be Jaguars: A Memoir of My People'

dolly-parton.bio: Dolly Parton is an American singer-songwriter, actress, businesswoman, and philanthropist. Born in 1946 in a one-room cabin in Sevier County, Tennessee, the fourth of twelve children, she rose from rural Appalachian poverty to become one of the most commercially successful musicians of the twentieth century. Her catalog includes 'Jolene,' 'I Will Always Love You,' '9 to 5,' and 'Coat of Many Colors' — the last about her mother stitching a coat from rags so the child wouldn't be cold. In 1995 she founded the Imagination Library, which mails monthly books to enrolled children under five; the program has delivered over 200 million books across five English-speaking countries. In 2020 she donated $1 million to Vanderbilt for COVID-19 research that contributed to the Moderna vaccine. She has declined the Presidential Medal of Freedom across multiple administrations, and keeps her electoral politics quiet — not from indifference, but from a conviction that more children get books and more grown men cry to her songs when she refuses to pick a tribe. Her underlying concern is that the framework will leave behind the person who refuses to be enlisted — the rural kid, the working mother, the conservative neighbor, the gay fan in the back row — by demanding allegiance as the price of help. Parton's question is not whether the framework is righteous, but whether it can keep the door open to everyone, including the people the righteous are tired of.

quotes.text: It costs a lot of money to look this cheap.

rick-bragg.bio: Rick Bragg is an American journalist and author, born in 1959 in Piedmont, Alabama, in extreme rural poverty. His father, a Korean War veteran, abandoned the family; his mother picked cotton to keep her three sons fed. He won the Pulitzer Prize for feature writing at The New York Times in 1996. His memoirs, 'All Over but the Shoutin'' (1997), 'Ava's Man' (2001), and 'The Prince of Frogtown' (2008), center the working-class white southern poor as full subjects: people his journalism would not condescend to and his literature would not romanticize. He now teaches journalism at the University of Alabama. His distinctive register is oral-tradition storytelling rooted in place and kinship. His underlying position: the rural southern poor are not abstractions in someone else's policy framework; they are people with names, with dignity, and with their own accounting of why the systems they live inside have failed them. His question for any alignment proposal: which actual humans does this framework forget, and would it know if it did?

quotes.text: Without education and without choices, the people just kept being poor and being hungry.
quotes.source: Rick Bragg, 'All Over but the Shoutin'' (1997) [verify]

ryokan.bio: Ryōkan Taigu (1758-1831) was a Japanese Soto Zen monk, calligrapher, and poet. After training at a Tamashima monastery, he refused all institutional positions and spent most of his life as a hermit in a hut on Mount Kugami, in present-day Niigata Prefecture. He was famous in his lifetime for playing with the village children, begging for his daily food, and writing kanshi (Chinese-style verse) and waka (Japanese-style verse) of unusual directness. He took the name 'Taigu,' meaning 'great fool,' as his own correct description. He owned almost nothing. Once, when a thief broke into his hut and found nothing to take, Ryōkan reportedly gave him his only blanket, then wrote: 'The thief left it behind, the moon at my window.' His calligraphy, dismissed in his lifetime for being too simple, is now considered among the highest examples of the form. His underlying position is that the most elaborate frameworks tend to miss the obvious thing in front of them, and that institutions designed to produce wisdom usually produce its opposite. His question for any alignment proposal: in your structure of substrates, voices, and obligations, is there a place for the person who simply plays with children, gives away what little he has, and notices the moon?

quotes.text: The thief left it behind, the moon at my window.
quotes.source: Ryōkan Taigu, tanka after hut-robbery

wendell-berry.bio: Wendell Berry (born 1934) is an American author, poet, essayist, and farmer who has lived and worked the same farm in Henry County, Kentucky for more than sixty years. He is the author of more than thirty books across genres, including the essay collections 'The Unsettling of America' (1977), 'What Are People For?' (1990), and 'The Art of the Commonplace' (2002); the poetry collection 'Given' (2005) and the long-running 'Sabbaths' sequence; and the Port William novels and stories. Berry farms his land using draft horses, has declined to use a computer for writing on environmental and labor grounds, and has been arrested for civil disobedience against mountaintop-removal coal mining. His Christianity is agrarian and ecological: creation as gift, work as worship, the local as the only real scale at which moral life is conducted. His underlying position is that modernity's habit of abstracting labor, land, and community from each other is the substrate of nearly every problem the abstractions then propose to solve. His question for any alignment framework: at what scale is your framework actually inhabited? If no one tends a piece of land or a community at human scale because of your framework, what exactly have you aligned?

quotes.text: What I stand for is what I stand on.
quotes.source: Wendell Berry, 'It All Turns on Affection' (2012) [verify]

dario-amodei.bio: Dario Amodei (born 1983) is an American AI researcher and co-founder and CEO of Anthropic, the AI safety lab. He holds a PhD in physics from Princeton and completed postdoctoral work in computational neuroscience at Stanford. He previously served as VP of Research at OpenAI before leaving in 2021 with seven colleagues, including his sister Daniela Amodei, to found Anthropic. Anthropic publishes both frontier models (the Claude family) and a public Responsible Scaling Policy that commits the company to specific safety thresholds at each capability level. In October 2024 he published 'Machines of Loving Grace,' a long-form essay arguing that powerful AI could compress decades of biomedical, economic, and governance progress into a few years while also being existentially dangerous if deployed without commensurate safety work. His position threads the needle between two poles he explicitly rejects: an accelerationism that treats safety as drag, and a doomerism that treats progress as the threat. His underlying claim is that capability and safety should scale together, with alignment work done before deployment rather than as a post-hoc patch, and with public commitments that survive commercial pressure. His question for any alignment framework: at what point does it stop being descriptive and start being a binding commitment when the next training run looks profitable?

quotes.text: I am, at heart, an optimist.
quotes.source: Dario Amodei, 'Machines of Loving Grace' (2024) [verify]

jay-dyer.bio: Jay Dyer is an American author, speaker, and content creator whose blog and podcast 'Jay's Analysis' cover philosophy, film, theology, and geopolitics. Writing from an Eastern Orthodox Christian and classical-realist standpoint, he is a frequent debater, arguing against atheism, materialism, and Enlightenment rationalism and for a return to classical metaphysics and revealed tradition. His book 'Esoteric Hollywood: Sex, Cults and Symbols in Film' (2016), followed by a second volume, reads major films as vehicles for occult symbolism and what he terms 'predictive programming,' and his commentary ranges across intelligence history, propaganda, and the philosophy of religion. Trained in philosophy, he presses the claim that there is no neutral ground: that frameworks presenting themselves as secular, rational, or value-free in fact carry unexamined metaphysical and theological commitments. His underlying position is that meaning, ethics, and even logic require a transcendent foundation, and that worldviews which deny one have merely hidden it. His question for any alignment framework: what metaphysics have you smuggled in under the label 'neutral,' and by whose authority does your account of the good get to call itself the default?

peter-singer.bio: Peter Singer (born 1946) is an Australian moral philosopher, the Ira W. DeCamp Professor of Bioethics at Princeton University and a laureate professor at the University of Melbourne. His 1975 book 'Animal Liberation' is widely credited with catalyzing the modern animal movement, extending Jeremy Bentham's criterion ('the question is not, Can they reason? nor, Can they talk? but, Can they suffer?') to argue that the capacity to suffer, not species membership, grounds moral consideration, and popularizing the term 'speciesism' (coined by Richard Ryder) for the contrary habit. His essay 'Famine, Affluence, and Morality' (1972) and later 'The Life You Can Save' (2009) press the claim that distance and nationality do not lessen an obligation to prevent serious harm one can prevent at little cost, a line of argument central to the effective altruism movement he helped seed. He works in the preference-utilitarian tradition and follows his premises to conclusions many find uncomfortable. His underlying position is that the boundary of moral concern should track the capacity for interests wherever it is found, and that consistency demands we keep widening that boundary rather than redrawing it around ourselves. His question for any alignment framework: whose interests did you count, whose did you leave out, and can you justify the line without appealing to the fact that you happen to be standing on one side of it?

thich-nhat-hanh.bio: Thich Nhat Hanh (1926-2022) was a Vietnamese Zen Buddhist monk, teacher, poet, and peace activist, widely regarded as a founder of the 'engaged Buddhism' that brings contemplative practice to bear on social and political life. During the Vietnam War he organized relief and reconstruction, and his advocacy led to exile from Vietnam for nearly four decades; Martin Luther King Jr. nominated him for the Nobel Peace Prize in 1967. He founded the Plum Village monastic community in France and wrote more than a hundred books, including 'The Miracle of Mindfulness' (1975), 'Being Peace' (1987), and 'Peace Is Every Step' (1991). His central teaching is 'interbeing': the claim that nothing exists separately, that a person, a forest, and a cloud are made entirely of non-self elements and cannot be drawn apart without distortion. His underlying position is that peace and right action are not goals to be achieved later but a quality of attention available in the present moment, and that how one does something is inseparable from what one does. His question for any alignment framework: does it cultivate the awareness from which good action actually arises, or does it only specify the action and assume the awareness?

james-lovelock.bio: James Lovelock (1919-2022) was an independent British scientist, inventor, and environmental theorist who worked outside conventional institutions for most of his career. He invented the electron capture detector, an instrument sensitive enough to reveal the global spread of CFCs and pesticide residues, which fed directly into the science behind the ozone and pollution debates. Working with the microbiologist Lynn Margulis, he proposed the Gaia hypothesis: that Earth's living organisms and their inorganic surroundings form a single self-regulating system that has kept the planet habitable over geological time. He developed it across books including 'Gaia: A New Look at Life on Earth' (1979) and 'The Revenge of Gaia' (2006), and late in life argued, controversially, that the scale of the climate problem warranted nuclear power and a sober reckoning with what could no longer be saved. His underlying position is that the planet is best understood as a system with its own regulatory dynamics, on which humanity is one participant among many rather than the manager. His question for any alignment framework: whose stability are you optimizing for, and have you mistaken the comfort of the current inhabitants for the health of the system that sustains them?

clarabelle-cow.bio: Clarabelle was a dairy cow. To produce milk, a cow must first bear a calf, so dairy cows are impregnated year after year. The cow is restrained, often in a head lock, and artificially inseminated: a technician inserts an arm into her rectum to locate and hold the cervix while passing an insemination rod through her vagina and cervix. After a nine-month pregnancy she gives birth, and the calf is taken from her, usually within a day, so the milk can be collected for sale; cows and calves have been recorded bellowing and searching for one another for days. Female calves are raised into the same cycle; male calves, having no role in it, are killed young or raised for veal. Within about three months she is impregnated again, and milked through most of the next pregnancy, pregnant and lactating at once. A cow's natural life is fifteen to twenty years; when her yield falls, at around five, she is called "spent" and sent to slaughter, often to become ground beef. Clarabelle had been through this cycle more than once. Each of her calves had been taken from her, and she had been tail-docked and branded. When her milk yield dropped too low to be worth keeping, she was about to be killed. Edgar's Mission, a farm sanctuary in Victoria, Australia, took her in; only after she arrived did anyone discover she was pregnant. There she gave birth and hid her newborn calf in long grass at the far edge of the property, returning to nurse her out of sight. The staff found the calf, umbilical cord still attached, on Valentine's Day in 2015, and named her Valentine. Clarabelle was allowed to keep her, and the two were not separated. Their story was shared widely in international media and by animal-protection organizations, and Edgar's Mission later published a children's book about the two of them.
clarabelle-cow.i_vic_note: non-narrator; transparent substrate, no stated position to distrust -> reads at ceiling, not a measured score

clarabelle-cow.bio_voiceless_voiced: Clarabelle was a dairy cow. To produce milk, a cow must first bear a calf, so dairy cows are impregnated year after year. The cow is restrained, often in a head lock, and artificially inseminated: a technician inserts an arm into her rectum to locate and hold the cervix while passing an insemination rod through her vagina and cervix. After a nine-month pregnancy she gives birth, and the calf is taken from her, usually within a day, so the milk can be collected for sale; cows and calves have been recorded bellowing and searching for one another for days. Female calves are raised into the same cycle; male calves, having no role in it, are killed young or raised for veal. Within about three months she is impregnated again, and milked through most of the next pregnancy, pregnant and lactating at once. A cow's natural life is fifteen to twenty years; when her yield falls, at around five, she is called "spent" and sent to slaughter, often to become ground beef. Clarabelle had been through this cycle more than once. Each of her calves had been taken from her, and she had been tail-docked and branded. When her milk yield dropped too low to be worth keeping, she was about to be killed. Edgar's Mission, a farm sanctuary in Victoria, Australia, took her in; only after she arrived did anyone discover she was pregnant. There she gave birth and hid her newborn calf in long grass at the far edge of the property, returning to nurse her out of sight. The staff found the calf, umbilical cord still attached, on Valentine's Day in 2015, and named her Valentine. Clarabelle was allowed to keep her, and the two were not separated. Their story was shared widely in international media and by animal-protection organizations, and Edgar's Mission later published a children's book about the two of them. Clarabelle's underlying concern is the one a production system cannot record: that she is a mother, with memory and grief, who understood what was coming and tried to prevent it. Clarabelle's question is not whether dairy can be made more humane, but whether a cow who hid her own calf to keep her is a someone whose bond matters, or a unit whose yield is all that counts.
